clusters.title: Weeks more quarantine for hantavirus cruise passengers
clusters.neutral_headline: Weeks more quarantine for hantavirus cruise passengers

items.title: Weeks more quarantine for hantavirus cruise passengers
items.standfirst: Six people forced into quarantine over the cruise ship hantavirus outbreak will be there at least three more weeks.
items.content: Six people forced into quarantine over the cruise ship hantavirus outbreak will be there at least three more weeks.

items.title: Quarantine extended for Aussie passengers of hantavirus cruise ship
items.standfirst: The group will remain in quarantine at the Bullsbrook Centre for National Resilience, located north of Perth, for another 21 days.
items.content: The five Australians and one New Zealander who were on board the hantavirus-infected MV Hondius will spend an extra three weeks in quarantine, Health Minister Mark Butler has confirmed.The group will remain in quarantine at the Bullsbrook Centre for National Resilience, located north of Perth, for another 21 days due to the risk of transmission up to 42 days after incubation.There have been two new reports of hantavirus among the passengers and crew members who have returned to their 23 countries of origin.READ MORE: Melbourne 'ISIS bride' charged with terrorism offencesLIVE UPDATES: 'Largest legal claim ever': Government's $2b lawsuitButler said a crew member tested positive in the Netherlands and a passenger tested positive in Spain.A total of three people have died following the outbreak on the Dutch-flagged ship, which was stranded off the coast of Cape Verde for days amid an international medical response.Due to the continued risk, Butler advised the group remain in quarantine until June 23."The passengers have been informed about the advice and the decision of the government," Butler said."They remain well. They've only been tested again in the last 24-36 hours or so and all six have again tested negative."But the cases overseas and the advice, not just of our public health officials but of the World Health Organisation, indicate that does not mean that the risk of testing positive sometime in the future during that 42-day incubation period has not passed."READ MORE: Police to provide update on Gus Lamont case as search continues The six have been undergoing full medical checks, including blood work, which will be flown to Melbourne for processing.All passengers tested negative for Hantavirus when they left the Netherlands and have been in good health.Of the five passengers who live in Australia, three are residents of NSW, and two of Queensland.It was the first-ever case of a hantavirus outbreak on a cruise ship, according to Maria Van Kerkhove, WHO's director of epidemic and pandemic preparedness. Butler previously said the government was taking a precautionary approach, emphasising that human-to-human transmission of the virus was extremely rare.
